name: Big O Notation: Visualizing Algorithmic Time Complexities
description:
This visualization compares the growth rates of common algorithmic time complexities, often referred to as Big O Notation. As the input size (n) increases, the difference in the number of operations required becomes staggering.

  • O(1) & O(log n): These represent highly efficient algorithms (e.g., hash table lookups or binary search) that scale exceptionally well.
  • O(n): Linear growth where time increases proportionally with data size (e.g., simple array iteration).
  • O(n log n): Typical of efficient sorting algorithms like Merge Sort or Quick Sort.
  • O(n²) & O(2ⁿ): These are computationally expensive. $O(n²)$ is common in nested loops, while $O(2ⁿ)$ represents exponential growth, often seen in recursive solutions for the traveling salesperson problem or brute-force searches.

Note: The y-axis is constrained to emphasize the 'knee' where inefficient algorithms become impractical for real-world datasets.
